region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent (South Asia); widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_description: Syzygium cumini — SNOMED CT substance concept for jambolan/jamun
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 415555003 refers to the plant substance Syzygium cumini; used as closest parent for the edible fruit.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of jamun is approximately 100 g of fresh whole berries, equivalent to about 10–12 individual fruits consumed as a snack or dessert.

nutrient_source.title: Jamun (Syzygium cumini) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (2017 edition), which lists Syzygium cumini (jamun) as a primary entry. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian foods.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of jamun (Syzygium cumini) — peer-reviewed evidence
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; corroborated by Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Jamun (Syzygium cumini) has a reported GI of approximately 25 (low), consistent with its low sugar content and high anthocyanin/polyphenol profile. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. GL per 100 g serving = (25 × 14.0) / 100 = 3.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (26 mg/100 g) and presence of potassium (79 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Jamun is low in sodium and provides modest potassium, making it a suitable fruit choice for individuals managing hypertension; portion sizes of 100–150 g are appropriate within a DASH-style dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 25) and bioactive anthocyanins with demonstrated anti-hyperglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Jamun has a very low GI of 25 and contains jamboline and anthocyanins that may help moderate postprandial blood glucose; it is considered a beneficial fruit for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard serving sizes of 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and high polyphenol antioxidant content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jamun is virtually fat-free and cholesterol-free, and its high anthocyanin and ellagic acid content may confer cardioprotective antioxidant effects; it can be freely included in a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jamun (Syzygium cumini), also known as Java plum, black plum, or Indian blackberry, is a tropical fruit native to the Indian subcontinent and widely cultivated across South and Southeast Asia, including Singapore's Indian community. The fruit is small, oval, and deep purple to black when ripe, with a thin edible skin and a single large seed. The flesh is juicy, astringent, and mildly sweet-sour, with a distinctive dark purple pigment that stains the tongue and lips. Jamun is eaten fresh as a seasonal snack, often sprinkled with salt or black salt (kala namak) to balance its tartness. It is also used to make juices, vinegar, wine, and traditional Ayurvedic preparations. Nutritionally, jamun is low in calories (62 kcal/100 g) and fat (0.3 g/100 g), with a moderate carbohydrate content (14 g/100 g) dominated by natural sugars (9.9 g/100 g). Its glycemic index is very low at approximately 25, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly fruits available. The fruit is rich in anthocyanins, ellagic acid, and jamboline — bioactive compounds that have demonstrated anti-hyperglycaemic, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties in clinical and preclinical studies. Jamun provides useful amounts of vitamin C (18 mg/100 g), iron (1.2 mg/100 g), and calcium (15 mg/100 g). In Singapore, jamun is available seasonally in Indian grocery stores and is served at Indian restaurants as a dessert fruit or digestive. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals following halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of jamun?
What is Java plum called in Tamil?
Is jamun the same as black plum?
What are the health benefits of jamun?

notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are -1 as these fatty acid fractions are not individually reported in the ICMR-NIN source for jamun. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as folate is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this fruit. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — raw fruit contains no industrial trans fats). Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived fruit). Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant food contains no B12). Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — negligible in tropical fruits not exposed to UV fortification). The input record listed food_group as 'Mixed'; corrected to 'Vegetables' (fruit subgroup) consistent with ICMR-NIN classification. Price data (0.01 SGD) from field survey input is implausible and has been excluded as it likely reflects a data entry error in the seed record. The cuisine tag 'Butter Chicken' and 'Curry' from the input are not applicable to jamun as a raw fruit and have been excluded. Jamun is served at Mr Tandoor - Boat Quay (field survey data source) likely as a dessert fruit or seasonal offering.
